fingerprint: The Roaring 40s latitude band (40-50°S) is a direct consequence of the Coriolis parameter f = 2Ω sin(φ) on a rotating sphere. The sin(φ) dependence — which concentrates the Ferrel cell westerlies at mid-latitudes — has no derivation from flat-disc geometry, where the vertical component of Ω is uniform across the surface. The dome's claimed mechanism ('aetheric rim pressure at the disc edge') would produce winds at the disc rim (r ≈ 20,015 km), not at a specific geographic latitude. The dome's own monitoring uses globe-derived WGS84 latitude (48.5°S) with no conversion to disc-radial coordinates.
globe_value: Coriolis parameter f = 2Ω sin(φ), producing westerly jet maximum at ~45°S where the meridional temperature gradient and Coriolis deflection balance. SAA position from spherical harmonic decomposition (IGRF/WMM) of geodynamo field in spherical liquid outer core.
dome_value: Claims 'aetheric rim pressure at disc edge' drives both Roaring 40s and SAA. No formula, no derivation, no computation in the repository. monitor.py Domain 27 hardcodes observed='22.5' with pass=True; Domain 33 hardcodes observed='48.5' with pass=True. Only Domain 17 (AAO index) fetches live data, but tests AAO > 0.3σ, not the causal mechanism.
match_precision: Latitude band 40-50°S adopted directly from globe meteorology. 48.5°S monitoring value is a WGS84 latitude with no disc-radial equivalent provided.
why_it_matters: On a rotating sphere, the Coriolis force has a latitude-dependent vertical component: f = 2Ω sin(φ). This sin(φ) variation is what creates distinct circulation cells (Hadley, Ferrel, Polar) at specific latitude bands. On a flat rotating disc, the angular velocity vector Ω is everywhere perpendicular to the surface — the Coriolis deflection is uniform at all radii (f = 2Ω, constant). A flat disc cannot produce latitude-banded wind patterns from rotational dynamics. The dome model claims 'aetheric rim pressure' drives the Roaring 40s, but provides no formula connecting disc_radius or firmament_height to the 40-50°S wind belt. The dome's code doesn't even attempt a derivation — two of three monitoring domains are hardcoded static wins. The SAA itself requires a geodynamo operating in a spherical liquid outer core shell; the dome's flat-disc geometry has no analogous dynamo structure.
suggested_text: The Roaring 40s wind belt at 40–50°S exists because the Coriolis parameter f = 2Ω sin(φ) varies with latitude on a rotating sphere, concentrating westerly momentum at mid-latitudes where the Ferrel cell's poleward airflow is deflected eastward. On a flat rotating disc, f = 2Ω everywhere — the vertical component of Earth's angular velocity is constant across the surface, and there is no sin(φ) variation to create distinct wind bands. The dome's 'aetheric rim pressure' mechanism provides no formula to explain why winds should peak at 40–50°S rather than at any other distance from the disc center. The dome's own monitor.py confirms the absence of any predictive computation: Domain 27 hardcodes observed='22.5' with pass=True, and Domain 33 hardcodes observed='48.5' with pass=True — static assertions that can never fail.

code_search.found_in: inject_ai_layer.py: WIN-024 title entry only ('Roaring 40s = SAA southern boundary', data_source 'Observations'). monitor.py: three domains (17, 27, 33). Domain 17 fetches AAO index from CPC/NOAA, checks aao > 0.3. Domain 27: observed='22.5', pass=True (hardcoded static win). Domain 33: observed='48.5', pass=True (hardcoded static win).
code_search.monitoring: partial — 1 of 3 domains uses live data (AAO index), 2 are hardcoded static wins
code_search.notes: No Coriolis computation, no rim-pressure formula, no disc-edge wind derivation exists anywhere in the repository. The dome's 'aetheric rim pressure' mechanism is purely verbal — no code implements it. The 48.5°S value is a WGS84 latitude used as-is with no conversion to dome disc coordinates.
